questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The evidence shows the paper pre-trained ViT on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also reports evaluation results on ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, with 87M parameters. The paper’s reference ViT/DeiT-B variant is 86M, but the largest reported variant in the evidence is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, which gives linear computational complexity with respect to image size and makes it suitable as a general-purpose vision backbone. The evidence also notes the use of relative position bias, while absolute position embedding slightly hurts dense prediction tasks.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, using centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper highlights that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 up to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so papers do not agree on a single value. There are also multiple SOTA claims with different numbers, including CoCa (finetuned) at 91.0, PaLI-17B at 90.9, and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51, indicating conflicting reported best results across papers.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The evidence does not list any explicit SOTA claims for CIFAR-100 in this subset.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus does not fully agree: the highest ADE20K mIoU in the evidence is 62.9 for InternImage-H, but another paper claims SOTA with ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is also reported at 59.9. So there are conflicting SOTA claims, with InternImage-H appearing best in this corpus.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The ADE20K mIoU values in the corpus vary widely, from 26.6 to 62.9 (spread 36.3, stddev 7.29), so they are not consistent. There are also conflicting SOTA claims across papers, with InternImage-H reported at 62.9, ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5, and SwinV2-G at 59.9.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No, the corpus does not agree. Some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design or necessary to add positional information, e.g. ViT says it "adds position embeddings" and CPVT says vision transformers need positional encodings because self-attention is permutation-equivariant. But other papers report they are not necessary or can be removed with little/no loss: CvT says "Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance," and the self-supervised ViT study says removing them "only slightly hurts performance."

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
